cross_reg_analysis.raw_md:
## 1. Cross-Regulation Analysis

> **Domain analysis:** [D-01.2 Data in Transit Encryption](../../DomainAnalysis/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md)

### D-01.2 Data in Transit Encryption

<!-- participants: GDPR, CRA, DORA; NIS2 partial via SR-NIS2-007; AI_Act absent -->

**Participants (from CRDA):** GDPR, CRA, DORA (NIS2 partially present via SR-NIS2-007 on secured voice/video/text communications — flagged as "out-of-scope for D-01.2 standalone" by the CRDA taxonomy; AI_Act not present per AEGIS taxonomy §4.1).

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ CRA

**GDPR article (verbatim — SR-GDPR-002, Art. 32(1)(b) + Art. 32(2)):**
> Art. 32(1)(b) the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services. Art. 32(2) in assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks … from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed.

**CRA article (verbatim — SR-CRA-003, Annex I Part I (2)(e) + (2)(j)):**
> Annex I Part I (2)(e) … by encrypting relevant data at rest or in transit using state-of-the-art mechanisms. Annex I Part I (2)(j) limit attack surfaces … including external interfaces.

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR: personal data in transit (Art. 32(2) "transmitted").
- CRA: data in transit through product with digital elements (personal or other).
- Scope overlap: Y when the CRA product transmits personal data.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR: continuous + on material change.
- CRA: continuous.
- Trigger alignment: SAME.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: "appropriate" anchored to Art. 32(2) risk events.
- CRA: "state-of-the-art mechanisms".
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: supervisory authority.
- CRA: MSA + ENISA.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: technical-and-organis measures + risk assessment of the five-event enumeration.
- CRA: transport-security profile in technical documentation (Annex VII §2(b)).

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY.

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME (CORRECTED — wording only).

**Reasoning:** The two impose the same in-transit confidentiality obligation on the same artefacts (channels transmitting personal data), with the same PR.DS-02 + PR.IR-01 CSF anchors. The CRA substrate is stricter (state-of-the-art) than GDPR's risk-anchored layer; meeting CRA discharges GDPR on the in-transit channel. The Annex I (2)(j) attack-surface-limitation leg is the additional CRA primitive (the in-transit control + surface limitation as a pair) that GDPR does not separately require.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Y when the same product transmits personal data; disjoint in baseline (CRA manufacturer ≠ GDPR controller).

**Downstream implication:** Stack-merge on the same channel; a CRA-compliant TLS configuration + network-segmentation profile discharges the GDPR Art. 32(1)(b)/(2) in-transit control.

---

#### Pair: GDPR ↔ DORA

**GDPR article (verbatim — SR-GDPR-002, Art. 32(1)(b)):**
> Art. 32(1)(b) the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services.

**DORA article (verbatim — SR-DORA-001, Art. 9(2)):**
> Art. 9(2) … high standards of availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, whether at rest, in use or in transit.

**Scope comparison:**
- GDPR: personal data in transit.
- DORA: data in transit of the financial entity (4×3 cell).
- Scope overlap: Y (conditional) when financial entity transmits personal data — typical.

**Trigger comparison:**
- GDPR: continuous.
- DORA: continuous.
- Trigger alignment: SAME.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- GDPR: "appropriate".
- DORA: "high standards".
- Tension: NONE.

**Recipient comparison:**
- GDPR: SA.
- DORA: CA + ESAs.

**Content template comparison:**
- GDPR: technical-and-organis measures.
- DORA: ICT security policies + protocols + tools.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY.

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME (CORRECTED — wording only).

**Reasoning:** The two obligations are the same on the in-transit cell of DORA's 4×3 matrix. OJ-explicit "in transit" in DORA vs risk-anchored in GDPR, but the operational substrate (mutual TLS, authenticated channels, network segmentation) is identical. A single in-transit design discharges both.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Y for the DORA financial entity processing personal data.

**Downstream implication:** Single-control — a mutually-authenticated encrypted-channel architecture + documented network-segmentation policy satisfies both.

---

#### Pair: CRA ↔ DORA

**CRA article (verbatim — SR-CRA-003, Annex I Part I (2)(e)+(j)):**
> Annex I Part I (2)(e) … encrypting relevant data at rest or in transit using state-of-the-art mechanisms. Annex I Part I (2)(j) limit attack surfaces.

**DORA article (verbatim — SR-DORA-001, Art. 9(2)):**
> Art. 9(2) … high standards of availability, authenticity, integrity and confidentiality of data, whether at rest, in use or in transit.

**Scope comparison:**
- CRA: product with digital elements (product-level).
- DORA: financial entity's ICT (entity-level).
- Scope overlap: Y (conditional) when a DORA financial entity uses a CRA-regulated product that transmits data.

**Trigger comparison:**
- CRA: continuous.
- DORA: continuous.
- Trigger alignment: SAME.

**Threshold/timeline comparison:**
- CRA: state-of-the-art + harm-surf-limit.
- DORA: high standards.
- Tension: NONE.

**Classified relationship (from CRDA):** COMPLEMENTARY.

**Verified relationship (after OJ-text analysis):** SAME (CORRECTED — wording only).

**Reasoning:** CRA product-side state-of-the-art in-transit + attack-surface-limitation is the floor; DORA entity-side adds the entity-level transport-security governance (zone trust, mTLS architecture, peer-peer authentication between internal services). PR.DS-02 + PR.IR-01 are shared anchors.

**Scope-disjoint test:** Y (DORA financial entity using CRA product); disjoint in baseline (manufacturer ≠ financial entity).

**Downstream implication:** Stack-merge — CRA product supplies the in-transit cipher-suite and surface-limitation baseline; DORA entity adds the deployment governance.

---

hso.raw_md:
## 2. Hierarchical Security Objective

> **CRDA-deep provenance.** This section was generated from
> [`../../CrossRegulation/DeepAnalysis/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md`](../../CrossRegulation/DeepAnalysis/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md)
> (3 pairs verified against OJ text).

### Participants (from CRDA-deep §D-01.2)

| Regulation | Canonical SO | 1-line summary | Scope/angle |
|---|---|---|---|
| GDPR | SO-GDPR-001 (cross-ref) | Confidentiality, integrity, resilience of processing systems — covers in-transit via Art. 32(2) "transmitted" | Controller/processor; Art. 32(1)(b) + Art. 32(2); personal-data scope; risk-anchored; PR.DS-02 + PR.IR-01 substrate |
| CRA | SO-CRA-001 (cross-ref) / SO-CRA-003 | Confidentiality of data in transit via state-of-the-art encryption + attack-surface limitation | Manufacturer; Annex I Part I (2)(e) + (2)(j); product-level; state-of-the-art baseline + attack-surface-limitation |
| DORA | SO-DORA-001 | CIA+A maintained across data states, including in transit (4×3 matrix) | Financial entity; Art. 9(2); ICT-system-level; `high standards`; OJ-explicit `in transit` dimension |

### Validated relationships (from CRDA-deep §D-01.2)

All 3 pairs verified by CRDA-deep as **SAME** (CRDA-deep corrected CRDA's
initial 3 COMPLEMENTARY classifications to SAME after OJ-text analysis).
The underlying obligation is the same (in-transit encryption to protect
confidentiality against network-position adversaries); the pairs differ
only in scope qualifier (`personal data` vs `product with digital
elements` vs `ICT of financial entity`) and threshold anchor
(`appropriate` vs `state of the art` vs `high standards`). The CRA
substrate is stricter (state-of-the-art + Annex I (2)(j)
attack-surface-limitation) than GDPR's risk-anchored layer; meeting CRA
discharges GDPR on the in-transit channel. CRDA-deep explicitly notes
(GDPR↔CRA pair) that a CRA-compliant TLS configuration +
network-segmentation profile discharges the GDPR Art. 32(1)(b)/(2)
in-transit control.

| | GDPR | CRA | DORA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | — | Y (CRA product transmits personal data — typical); disjoint in baseline (manufacturer ≠ controller) | Y (DORA financial entity transmits personal data — typical) |
| **CRA** | Y | — | Y (DORA financial entity uses CRA product; disjoint in baseline as manufacturer ≠ financial entity) |
| **DORA** | Y | Y | — |

Legend: **Y** = scope overlap on a single channel (high-level SO can
engage on a single channel); **disjoint in baseline** = manufacturer and
controller/financial entity are typically different parties.

### Cross-recipient map

N/A — D-01.2 (data in transit encryption) does not involve distinct
recipient classes (same as D-01.1). The recipients are supervisory
authorities for compliance demonstration on inspection only. The
D-04.3 sub-domain is the canonical recipient-fragmentation locus for
the data-protection area.

---

### D-01.2.0 — High-level SecurityObjective

```yaml
id: SO-D-01.2.HL
subdomain: D-01.2
applies_to: auto  # activates when ≥2 applicable regs overlap on same channel
status: static
derivation_source: ../../CrossRegulation/DeepAnalysis/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md §D-01.2
verified_relationship_basis: CRDA-deep verified (3 pairs, all SAME — CRDA initial 3 COMPLEMENTARY corrected to SAME after OJ-text analysis)
emergent_tensions: 0
```

**Objective.** Data in transit — across network paths, inter-service
calls, and third-party APIs — is protected against unauthorised disclosure
and alteration through cryptographic confidentiality and integrity controls
(mutual authentication, encryption, integrity verification).

**Considerations.**
- The three participating regulations converge on the same substantive
  obligation (in-transit confidentiality) but each anchors it to a distinct
  scope and threshold: GDPR's `appropriate` (risk-anchored, personal-data
  scope, five-event Art. 32(2) risk enumeration), CRA's `state of the art`
  (product-level, harmonised standards + attack-surface-limitation Annex I
  (2)(j)), and DORA's `high standards` (ICT-system-level, OJ-explicit `in
  transit` dimension).
- CRDA-deep verified all 3 pairs as SAME — a single in-transit design
  (mutual TLS, authenticated channels, network segmentation) discharges all
  three obligations on the same channel.
- D-01.2 is a single-control sub-domain — in-transit is the cheapest of the
  three encryption sub-domains and modern TLS configurations typically
  satisfy all three without dedicated additional investment.
- The high-level SO describes the common obligation; the per-regulation
  perspective is preserved in the sub-SOs.

### Sub-domain scope structure (verified by CRDA-deep)

| Axis (reg) | Trigger | Material scope | Recipient | Legal anchor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GDPR** | continuous + on material change | personal data transmitted across networks | supervisory authority (DPA) | Art. 32(1)(b) + Art. 32(2) |
| **CRA** | continuous product-side baseline | data transmitted by product with digital elements | MSA + ENISA | Annex I Part I (2)(e) + (2)(j) |
| **DORA** | continuous | data in transit of the financial entity | national CA + ESAs | Art. 9(2) |

### Scope-overlap matrix (3×3)

(See the validated-relationships table above for the 3×3 matrix.)

### Scope-overlap principle (operational). The high-level SO
(`SO-D-01.2.HL`) engages on a given channel **if and only if ≥2
applicable regulations' scopes overlap on the same channel** (e.g. a
mutual-TLS connection between a GDPR controller's service and a
CRA-regulated product backend deployed by a DORA financial entity —
typical in modern SaaS stacks). Pure co-applicability on disjoint
channels does **not** trigger cross-regulation aggregation. For a 5-FTE
SaaS startup with GDPR + CRA applicability, the high-level SO engages
on the same channel when both apply (typical); DORA's participation is
limited to financial entities.

### Aggregation logic (from CRDA-deep verified classifications)

- 3 of 5 regulations participate (GDPR, CRA, DORA; NIS 2 is
  out-of-scope for D-01.2 standalone per CRDA taxonomy T3-vs-text gap —
  SR-NIS2-007 covers secured voice/video/text communications only; AI
  Act is out-of-scope per AEGIS taxonomy §4.1).
- 3 pairwise cells, verified by CRDA-deep as **all SAME** after
  OJ-text analysis: GDPR↔CRA (CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY),
  GDPR↔DORA (CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY), CRA↔DORA (CORRECTED from
  COMPLEMENTARY).
- The high-level SO describes the **COMMON OBJECTIVE** (in-transit
  cryptographic confidentiality + integrity); the **per-regulation
  perspective** (threshold anchor, scope qualifier, recipient, content
  template) is preserved verbatim in the three sub-SOs.
- The high-level SO does **NOT** resolve any conditional tension —
  there are 0 conditional emergent tensions in this sub-domain;
  CRDA-deep verified all 3 pairs as SAME. Phase 1C Doc 07 does not
  need to resolve contradictions on D-01.2 because none exist; the
  task is to document the **single in-transit control** that
  discharges all three obligations.
- **Scope caveat (CRITICAL)** — derived from CRDA-deep verified
  classifications: the high-level SO does NOT aggregate across
  manufacturer/controller hands-offs. A SaaS-using-CRA-product
  scenario has disjoint baseline obligations (CRA manufacturer supplies
  transport-security profile in technical documentation; GDPR
  controller implements in-transit control on its own channels); the
  two are separate compliance tracks unless the same party is both
  manufacturer and controller. The CRA product baseline does not by
  itself discharge the GDPR controller's deployment-side responsibility.

---

### D-01.2.1 — Sub-SO for GDPR

```yaml
id: SO-D-01.2.GDPR
subdomain: D-01.2
applies_to: [GDPR]
inherits_from: SO-GDPR-001  # cross-ref — D-01.2 is covered by SO-GDPR-001 (D-01.1) per per-reg SO file
source_SR: SR-GDPR-002
activation: if GDPR in applicable_regs
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: SAME (CRDA-deep, D-01.2: GDPR↔CRA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY; GDPR↔DORA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY)
```

**Objective.** Personal data transmitted across networks, inter-service
channels, and third-party infrastructure remains confidential and
unaltered in transit, with the in-transit protection anchored to the
five-event Art. 32(2) risk enumeration (accidental or unlawful
destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access
to, personal data transmitted) (Art. 32(1)(b) + Art. 32(2)).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-GDPR-002` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.DS-02, PR.IR-01.

---

### D-01.2.2 — Sub-SO for CRA

```yaml
id: SO-D-01.2.CRA
subdomain: D-01.2
applies_to: [CRA]
inherits_from: SO-CRA-003
source_SR: SR-CRA-003
activation: if CRA in applicable_regs
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: SAME (CRDA-deep, D-01.2: GDPR↔CRA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY; CRA↔DORA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY)
```

**Objective.** Where data is transmitted by a product with digital
elements — personal or other — the confidentiality of the data in transit
is protected by encrypting relevant data using state-of-the-art mechanisms,
with the Annex I (2)(j) attack-surface-limitation leg constraining the
exposed interfaces through which the data could otherwise leak (Annex I
Part I (2)(e) + (2)(j)).

**Considerations.**
- The CRA state-of-the-art + attack-surface-limitation substrate is stricter
  than GDPR's risk-anchored layer; meeting CRA discharges GDPR on the
  in-transit channel for the same artefact.
- The (2)(e)+(2)(j) combination operationalises the in-transit cell of the
  Annex I umbrella; PR.DS-02 + PR.IR-01 are the joint anchors, with
  PR.IR-01 capturing the attack-surface-limitation leg.

**CSF anchors (from `SR-CRA-003` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.DS-02, PR.IR-01.

---

### D-01.2.3 — Sub-SO for DORA

```yaml
id: SO-D-01.2.DORA
subdomain: D-01.2
applies_to: [DORA]
inherits_from: SO-DORA-001
source_SR: SR-DORA-001
activation: if DORA in applicable_regs
phase_1A_role: 1_of_N
verified_relationship: SAME (CRDA-deep, D-01.2: GDPR↔DORA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY; CRA↔DORA CORRECTED from COMPLEMENTARY)
```

**Objective.** Data confidentiality, integrity, authenticity and
availability are maintained at high standards across all data states,
including in transit, through ICT security policies, procedures, protocols
and tools (Art. 9(2)).

**Considerations.**
- DORA is OJ-explicit on the `in transit` dimension; the entity-level
  transport-security governance (zone trust, mTLS architecture,
  peer-peer authentication between internal services) is the DORA-specific
  overlay above the CRA product-side baseline.
- For a DORA financial entity that is also a GDPR controller, a single
  mutually-authenticated encrypted-channel architecture + documented
  network-segmentation policy satisfies both (CRDA-deep GDPR↔DORA pair
  reasoning).

**CSF anchors (from `SR-DORA-001` v0.3 security_rationale):**
PR.DS-01, PR.DS-02, PR.DS-10, PR.DS-12.
*(The PR.DS-01 / PR.DS-10 / PR.DS-12 anchors are inherited from the 4×3
matrix context; D-01.2 is the in-transit cell, which is anchored
specifically by PR.DS-02.)*

---

### Emergent tensions (from CRDA-deep §D-01.2)

None — sub-domain is **all-SAME in baseline (3 pairs verified as SAME
after OJ-text analysis; CRDA initial 3 COMPLEMENTARY classifications
corrected to SAME)**. No conditional emergent tensions. The threshold
divergence (GDPR `appropriate`, CRA `state of the art`, DORA `high
standards`) is wording-only on a common substantive obligation; the
operational convergence is on **mutually-authenticated encrypted channels
with network segmentation + attack-surface discipline**, and the
strictest floor (CRA state-of-the-art + Annex I (2)(j)
attack-surface-limitation) discharges the others on a single channel.
CRDA-deep explicitly notes (in the GDPR↔CRA pair reasoning) that a
CRA-compliant TLS configuration + network-segmentation profile
discharges the GDPR Art. 32(1)(b)/(2) in-transit control.

### Downstream implication for Phase 1C Doc 07

A company with ≥2 applicable regulations in scope of D-01.2 — with
overlapping scopes on the same channel — operates a **single in-transit
design (mutual TLS + network segmentation + attack-surface
discipline)** that discharges all obligations. The high-level SO
(`SO-D-01.2.HL`) describes the common obligation; the per-reg
perspective is preserved in the sub-SOs. **No conflict-resolution is
required on D-01.2 because CRDA-deep verified all 3 pairs as SAME.**
Phase 1C Doc 07 documents the **single-control strategy** — typically
satisfied with no additional investment for a company with modern TLS
configurations. For proportionality, in-transit is the cheapest of the
three encryption sub-domains; the 5-FTE SaaS default already covers
the three regulators without dedicated additional investment.

security_requirements.raw_md:
## 3. Security Requirements

> **Provenance.** Derived from `../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md`. See that file for cross-reg verified relationships, scope overlap matrix, Objective/Considerations content, and aggregation logic. This file adds Volere shell fields per the `../Volere_shell.md` template.

## Participating regulations

GDPR, CRA, DORA (3 of 5 regs; NIS 2 out-of-scope per T3-vs-text gap; AI Act out-of-scope per AEGIS taxonomy §4.1). CRDA-deep verified 3 pairs as all-SAME (3 corrected from COMPLEMENTARY after OJ-text analysis).

---

## High-level requirement

```yaml
- req_id: 1.2
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: integrity
  title: "Data in transit is protected by cryptographic confidentiality and integrity (multi-reg aggregation)"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-01.2.HL
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-01.2.0"

  description: |
    The product shall protect data in transit — across network paths, inter-service calls, and third-party APIs — against unauthorised disclosure and alteration through cryptographic confidentiality and integrity controls (mutual authentication, encryption, integrity verification), as the common substantive obligation converging across GDPR Art. 32(1)(b) + Art. 32(2), CRA Annex I Part I (2)(e) + (2)(j), and DORA Art. 9(2) where ≥2 of these scopes overlap on the same channel.

  fit_criterion: |
    TLS 1.3 (or stronger) is applied to all in-transit channels; mutual authentication (mTLS) is enforced where both channel endpoints authenticate to each other; certificate validation (chain, revocation, expiry) is configured to fail closed; network segmentation policy is documented; the strictest applicable floor (CRA state-of-the-art + Annex I (2)(j) attack-surface-limitation) discharges GDPR and DORA on a single channel; verified by automated network scan (cipher-suite enumeration + protocol-version probe) + manual configuration review + joint risk-assessment artefact citing all three legal anchors when scopes overlap.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: []

  nist_csf: [PR.DS-02]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [GDPR, CRA, DORA]
    scope_overlap: conditional   # HL engages only when ≥2 applicable regs overlap on same channel (see HSO scope-overlap principle)

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The three participating regulations converge on the same substantive obligation
    (in-transit confidentiality) but each anchors it to a distinct scope and
    threshold: GDPR's `appropriate` (risk-anchored, personal-data scope, five-event
    Art. 32(2) risk enumeration), CRA's `state of the art` (product-level, harmonised
    standards + attack-surface-limitation Annex I (2)(j)), and DORA's `high standards`
    (ICT-system-level, OJ-explicit `in transit` dimension). CRDA-deep verified all
    3 pairs as SAME — a single in-transit design (mutual TLS, authenticated channels,
    network segmentation) discharges all three obligations on the same channel.
    D-01.2 is a single-control sub-domain — in-transit is the cheapest of the three
    encryption sub-domains and modern TLS configurations typically satisfy all three
    without dedicated additional investment. The high-level SO describes the common
    obligation; the per-regulation perspective is preserved in the sub-SOs. (Copied
    from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-01.2.0`; preserved here for
    self-contained traceability.)
```

---

## Sub-requirements

### 1.2.1 — Personal data in transit

```yaml
- req_id: 1.2.1
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: integrity

  title: "Personal data in transit remains confidential and unaltered"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-01.2.GDPR
    per_reg_so: SO-GDPR-001  # cross-ref — D-01.2 is covered by SO-GDPR-001 (D-01.1) per per-reg SO file
    source_sr: SR-GDPR-002
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-01.2.1"

  description: |
    The product shall ensure that personal data transmitted across networks, inter-service channels, and third-party infrastructure remains confidential and unaltered in transit, with the in-transit protection anchored to the five-event Art. 32(2) risk enumeration (accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data transmitted) (GDPR Art. 32(1)(b) + Art. 32(2)).

  fit_criterion: |
    TLS 1.3 (or stronger) is applied to all in-transit channels carrying personal data; the five-event Art. 32(2) risk enumeration is addressed by technical measures on each event class (encryption for confidentiality, integrity verification for alteration, network segmentation for destruction/loss/access); cipher-suite selection is documented; verified by automated network scan + configuration audit + DPA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: ["1.3 (Key/Certificate Management — pending generation)", "3.x (Mutual Authentication — pending generation)"]

  nist_csf: [PR.DS-02, PR.IR-01]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [GDPR]
    scope_overlap: Y   # GDPR↔CRA = Y (CRA product transmits personal data — typical); GDPR↔DORA = Y (DORA FE transmits personal data — typical); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    HSO D-01.2.1 carries no separate Considerations block — the GDPR in-transit
    sub-SO is cross-referenced to SO-GDPR-001 (the D-01.1 SO) and inherits the
    D-01.1 GDPR Considerations by reference via SR-GDPR-002. (Recorded here
    as a traceability note; no per-reg perspective was omitted.)
```

### 1.2.2 — Product data in transit (state-of-the-art + attack-surface-limitation)

```yaml
- req_id: 1.2.2
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: integrity

  title: "Product-transmitted data is encrypted with state-of-the-art mechanisms and exposed interfaces are minimised"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-01.2.CRA
    per_reg_so: SO-CRA-003
    source_sr: SR-CRA-003
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-01.2.2"

  description: |
    The product shall protect the confidentiality of data in transit by encrypting the relevant data using state-of-the-art mechanisms, in a manner that renders the data unintelligible to any person not authorised to access it, and shall limit the attack surface of the product with digital elements by constraining the exposed interfaces through which the data could otherwise leak (CRA Annex I Part I (2)(e) + (2)(j)).

  fit_criterion: |
    State-of-the-art symmetric encryption (e.g. TLS 1.3 with AES-256-GCM or equivalent, harmonised-standards-conformant under CRA Art. 27) is applied to data in transit of the product with digital elements; the Annex I (2)(e) `render unintelligible` test is met; the Annex I (2)(j) attack-surface-limitation leg is documented (exposed interfaces enumerated, default ports minimised, unused services disabled); both internal east-west traffic and externally-exposed transport edges are covered per the R3 reading; verified by Annex I conformity-assessment evidence + technical documentation inspection + MSA/ENISA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: ["1.3 (Key/Certificate Management — pending generation)", "3.x (Mutual Authentication — pending generation)"]

  nist_csf: [PR.DS-02, PR.IR-01]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [CRA]
    scope_overlap: Y   # CRA↔GDPR = Y (CRA product transmits personal data — typical); CRA↔DORA = Y (DORA FE uses CRA product); HL engages on overlap (disjoint in baseline as manufacturer ≠ controller/FE)

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The CRA state-of-the-art + attack-surface-limitation substrate is stricter
    than GDPR's risk-anchored layer; meeting CRA discharges GDPR on the in-transit
    channel for the same artefact. The (2)(e)+(2)(j) combination operationalises
    the in-transit cell of the Annex I umbrella; PR.DS-02 + PR.IR-01 are the joint
    anchors, with PR.IR-01 capturing the attack-surface-limitation leg. (Copied
    from the HSO Considerations block of `### D-01.2.2`; preserved here for
    self-contained traceability.)
```

### 1.2.3 — Financial-entity ICT data in transit

```yaml
- req_id: 1.2.3
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: integrity

  title: "ICT-supported data of financial entity maintained at high standards of CIA in transit"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-D-01.2.DORA
    per_reg_so: SO-DORA-001
    source_sr: SR-DORA-001
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-01_Data-Protection/D-01.2.md
    objective_ref_section: "### D-01.2.3"

  description: |
    The product shall maintain data confidentiality, integrity, authenticity and availability at high standards across all data states, including in transit, through ICT security policies, procedures, protocols and tools (DORA Art. 9(2)).

  fit_criterion: |
    High-standards in-transit cryptography (TLS 1.3 or stronger, with cipher suites conforming to EBA/ESMA/EIOPA RTS under DORA Art. 15 where applicable) is applied across all in-transit channels of the financial entity's ICT landscape; mutual-authentication (mTLS) architecture + zone-trust + peer-peer authentication between internal services is documented; network-segmentation policy is documented; verified by configuration audit + zone-trust architecture review + national-CA + ESA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: ["1.3 (Key/Certificate Management — pending generation)", "3.x (Mutual Authentication — pending generation)"]

  nist_csf: [PR.DS-02]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [DORA]
    scope_overlap: Y   # DORA↔GDPR = Y (DORA FE transmits personal data — typical); DORA↔CRA = Y (FE uses CRA product); HL engages on overlap (disjoint in baseline as manufacturer ≠ financial entity)

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    DORA is OJ-explicit on the `in transit` dimension; the entity-level
    transport-security governance (zone trust, mTLS architecture, peer-peer
    authentication between internal services) is the DORA-specific overlay above
    the CRA product-side baseline. For a DORA financial entity that is also a
    GDPR controller, a single mutually-authenticated encrypted-channel
    architecture + documented network-segmentation policy satisfies both
    (CRDA-deep GDPR↔DORA pair reasoning). (Copied from the HSO Considerations
    block of `### D-01.2.3`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

---

## Decisions (CORR-029)

### Orphan SR justifications

**NIS2**: 1 SR (SR-NIS2-007) targets D-01.2 but no NIS2 SO exists. Per source MD line 38, NIS2 is 'partially present via SR-NIS2-007 on secured voice/video/text communications — flagged as out-of-scope for D-01.2 standalone by the CRDA taxonomy'. The secured-communications obligation is preserved as a partial-coverage cross-ref. No standalone NIS2 SO for D-01.2 is needed because the data-in-transit obligation does not extend to internal voice/video/text comms (those are in D-03.2 Communications Security). CORR-030 may re-anchor SR-NIS2-007 to D-03.2.
